nuance: Concedes a fact, then contradicts the expectation it sets up — bookish, a step above けど.

examples.en: Although I started speaking in Japanese, the words wouldn't come out.
Although I got the informal job offer, I still haven't decided on a place to live.

connection: V-stem / adj / N + ながらも
nuance: Two things hold at once against expectation — 'even while being X, still Y'.

examples.en: It's a small but cozy room.
Despite the heavy overtime, I study Japanese every day.

nuance: Literary 'while' — two simultaneous actions by one subject, stiffer than 〜ながら.

examples.en: I drank my morning coffee while gazing at the cherry blossoms outside the window.
I walked through the alleys while checking the map.

nuance: A change steadily underway toward completion — 'is coming to X', more formal than 〜ている.

examples.en: My Japanese is gradually improving, little by little.
It seems the rainy season is coming to an end.

nuance: Grants a hypothetical for argument's sake — 'even if we suppose X, still Y'.

examples.en: Even supposing I miss the last train, I can walk home.
Even supposing the rent is high, I want to live near this station.

connection: N / plain form + にしても
nuance: Grants a point but pushes back — 'even taking X into account, it's too much / still Y'.

examples.en: Even considering it's my first business trip, I'm way underprepared.
Even considering it gets crowded, the morning Yamanote Line is brutal.

connection: N / plain form + にしては
nuance: Result clashes with the standard the topic implies — 'for an X, surprisingly Y'.

examples.en: I was told my keigo is good for a foreigner.
It was a cool day, considering it's June.

nuance: Given X is now settled, a firm resolve or obligation follows — 'now that…, of course Y'.

examples.en: Now that I've come to Tokyo, I want to see all the festivals.
So long as I've taken it on, I'll see it through to the end.

nuance: Pins a bad outcome on one small cause — 'just because of X, unfortunately Y'.

examples.en: Simply because I took the wrong road, I ended up late for the interview.
Simply because I misread the kanji, I got off at the wrong station.

connection: plain form / N + ばかりか
nuance: Escalates past the first item to a stronger one — 'not just X, but even Y on top'.

examples.en: The store manager remembered not only my name but even my hometown.
This share house is not only cheap but even close to the station.

connection: N / plain form + のみならず
nuance: Formal, written 'not only X but also Y' — the bookish sibling of 〜だけでなく.

examples.en: This scheme concerns not only international students but also workers.
Tsukiji is popular not only with tourists but also with locals.

nuance: Piles a same-direction second point onto the first — 'not only that, and also'.

examples.en: On top of a long overtime shift today, I also missed the last train.
This room gets good sunlight, and on top of that it's quiet.

nuance: Do the second thing on the basis of having first done X — a deliberate, ordered step.

examples.en: I want to decide on the contract only after viewing the place.
I'll go to the ward office after I've checked the documents.

nuance: Sets two contrasting facts side by side — 'X, while on the other hand Y'.

examples.en: The city center is convenient, while on the other hand the rent is high.
My Japanese conversation has improved, whereas kanji is still my weak point.

nuance: A trend moving in one direction only, usually worsening — 'just keeps getting more X'.

examples.en: As summer approaches, the temperature just keeps on rising.
My savings just keep dwindling, so I'd better start working soon.

nuance: Two opposite sides of one thing — a pro paired with its con.

examples.en: The night shift pays well, but on the other side it's hard on the body.
City life has plenty of stimulation, while at the same time it's tiring.

nuance: Two things coincide, or one triggers the other the instant it happens.

examples.en: The moment the doors opened, the passengers got off.
At the same time as joining the company, I rented a new place.

nuance: Just as you registered X, the unexpected Y already followed — often surprise or reversal.

examples.en: No sooner had it cleared up than it started raining again.
No sooner had he come home than he left for work again.

nuance: Do the next thing the instant X is done — formal, common in business notices.

examples.en: I'll get in touch as soon as the interview result comes in.
I can move in as soon as a room opens up.

nuance: The outcome hinges entirely on X — 'it all depends on X'.

examples.en: I'll decide the weekend plans depending on the weather.
Depending on your effort, your Japanese can improve without limit.

nuance: Cites an extreme case to imply the rest — literary, often paired with a negative.

examples.en: When I'd just arrived, I couldn't even say my own address.
I'm so busy I don't even have time for lunch.

nuance: A wish framed as barely-possible — 'if it were even possible to X (which it isn't really)'.

examples.en: If only I could go back, I'd return to the day I first arrived in Tokyo.
If only I could, I'd go to Hokkaido right now.

nuance: The result holds firm so long as X does not happen — 'unless X, Y stays true'.

examples.en: Unless a typhoon comes, the fireworks festival will be held.
Unless I have a guarantor, I can't rent this room.

nuance: So long as the stated condition continues, the result holds — a standing boundary.

examples.en: As long as I live in Tokyo, my train-based lifestyle will continue.
As long as I'm healthy, I want to keep traveling while I work.

connection: N / plain form + だけあって
nuance: The result fits the reputation or status — 'befitting X, no wonder Y'. Admiring.

examples.en: As expected of a popular restaurant, the queue is huge.
As might be expected after studying every day, I passed the exam.

nuance: The very fact of X makes the result stronger or more natural — 'being X, all the more Y'.

examples.en: Precisely because I'd been looking forward to it, the cancellation was a letdown.
Precisely because it was my first winter, Tokyo's cold really got to me.

connection: N + から + N + にかけて
nuance: Marks the far end of a rough span (from A through B) — the range is approximate, not a precise deadline.

examples.en: From June into July, the rainy season keeps going.
From Friday into Sunday this week, heavy snow is forecast in Tokyo.

nuance: Stresses the whole extent of a large range — time, area, or scope. Formal; the noun names the span (three years, the whole country).

examples.en: I worked in Tokyo over the span of three years.
The typhoon's effects spread across a wide area.

nuance: Two senses: a means/intermediary (via an app, through a friend) or a whole period/extent (throughout the year).

examples.en: Through a friend's introduction, I found my current share house.
Throughout the whole year, Tokyo has many tourists.

nuance: Makes the noun the focal point around which the rest is organized — activity, movement, or discussion revolves around it.

examples.en: Centered on Shibuya, shops for young people are clustered together.
In the meeting, we talked mainly about next year's budget.

nuance: Names one prominent representative example, implying many more of the same kind follow.

examples.en: Starting with Asakusa, Tokyo has many famous tourist spots.
Led by the store manager, all the staff are kind.

nuance: A stiffer, more written 〜について — good for topics of inquiry, reports, and formal statements.

examples.en: I asked at the ward office regarding the renewal of my residence card.
If you have questions regarding the new contract, please email me.

nuance: Adds a second item on top of a first — often escalating (not just A, but also B).

examples.en: In addition to the rent, I also have to pay the utility bills.
Today, in addition to the wind, the rain is heavy too.

nuance: The result runs against an expectation, prediction, or rule. Formal; noun is usually 予想・期待・予測.

examples.en: Contrary to my expectations, the interview went well.
Contrary to the forecast, it was sunny all day.

nuance: Acts on the basis of a rule, data, or principle — the noun is the foundation the action rests on.

examples.en: I pay the rent based on the contract.
Based on the survey results, we changed the menu.

nuance: The noun is the contested subject that people argue, compete, or negotiate over. News-register.

examples.en: Residents are debating over the construction of the new station.
Employees and the company are at odds over overtime pay.

nuance: Physical (along a river/road) or abstract (in line with a plan or wishes) — following the shape of the noun.

examples.en: I rode my bike along the river.
I'll make the plan in line with the company's policy.

nuance: A written, formal 〜で marking the place, time, or field of an event. Common in announcements and academic prose.

examples.en: The onboarding ceremony will be held at the venue.
In modern Japan, the labor shortage is a problem.

nuance: The noun-modifying form of 〜において — attaches directly to a following noun (the situation in X).

examples.en: Life in Tokyo is convenient.
The use of English in the workplace is increasing.

nuance: Two senses: 'together with' (accompaniment) and 'as X happens, Y also changes' (parallel change).

examples.en: Together with my colleagues, I went to watch the fireworks.
As the times changed, ways of working changed too.

nuance: A change in one thing brings a proportional change in another. More formal/written than とともに.

examples.en: As the population increases, rents are rising too.
Accompanying the move, an address change is required.

nuance: Covered in or riddled with something unpleasant — mud, dust, mistakes. Always negative.

examples.en: Right after the move, the room was full of cardboard boxes.
The documents I submitted were full of mistakes.

nuance: Leaving something in a state you should have undone — often careless (left the light on) or nonstop (stood the whole time).

examples.en: I went out and left the light on.
On the packed train I stood the whole hour.

nuance: A soft, formal 'I can't / I'm not in a position to' — used to decline politely without a blunt 'no'.

examples.en: The staff are unable to answer that question.
I'm not in a position to decide this on my own.

nuance: A bad outcome is quite possible — 'could well end up …'. Only for negative, unwanted results.

examples.en: If you overdo it, you might well ruin your health.
If I oversleep, I could well be late for the interview.

connection: V-stem + 得る/得ない(えない)
nuance: Marks something as possible in principle. Formal/written; the negative is 得ない (えない).

examples.en: An earthquake can happen at any time.
That plan is a fully possible scenario.

nuance: There's no possible method to do the action — you'd do it if you could, but no means exists.

examples.en: I don't know the number, so there's no way to contact them.
I don't know the street name, so there's no way to explain it.

connection: N / plain form + に過ぎない
nuance: Plays something down as merely X and no bigger — just a trainee, just a guess.

examples.en: Right now I'm nothing more than a trainee.
That's nothing more than my own guess.

connection: plain form + とは限らない
nuance: Denies that a plausible generalization always holds — X isn't guaranteed just because it usually seems so.

examples.en: An expensive restaurant isn't necessarily always tasty.
Not every Japanese person can necessarily use polite speech perfectly.

meaning: it's natural that / should / used to
nuance: States a general truth, social expectation, or fond recollection — the way things naturally are or were.

examples.en: Back in my student days I used to miss the last train a lot.
When you're in trouble, people should help one another.

meaning: as if I would ever! (strong denial)
nuance: A rhetorical refusal — there's no way I'd ever do that. Blunt and emphatic; ものか in casual speech, ものですか softened.

examples.en: As if I'd ever go to that crowded shop again!
As if I'd ever want to ride such a packed train every day!

nuance: Logical impossibility — given the facts, that outcome simply cannot be. Stronger and more reasoned than はずがない.

examples.en: There's no way I can read every kanji after just one year.
There's no way he'd forget the promise.

connection: plain form / N + に決まっている
nuance: Speaker's confident conviction — it's obvious, of course it is. Subjective certainty, not proven fact.

examples.en: Tokyo in June is bound to be muggy.
If I don't book now it's definitely going to be fully booked.

nuance: Only one option remains — resigned, there's nothing else for it.

examples.en: I missed the last train, so I have no choice but to walk home.
The trains stopped for the typhoon, so I have no choice but to wait at home.

connection: V-ない stem + ざるを得ない (する→せざる)
nuance: Formal, reluctant obligation — forced by circumstance to do it against one's will. Written/stiff register.

examples.en: Because of the typhoon, we cannot help but cancel the trip.
If the boss asks, I cannot help but work overtime.

connection: V-ない stem + ずにはいられない
nuance: An impulse too strong to resist — you simply can't stop yourself from doing it.

examples.en: When I recall the Hokkaido sea, I can't help wanting to go back.
When the cherry blossoms bloom, I can't help taking photos.

connection: V-て / adj-くて + ならない
nuance: A feeling wells up beyond control — typically worry, curiosity, or emotion, slightly literary.

examples.en: I can't help worrying about the result of my residence card renewal.
I miss my friends back home unbearably.

connection: V-て / adj-くて + たまらない
nuance: A physical sensation or desire so intense it's unbearable — hot, sleepy, itchy, dying to do something.

examples.en: The rainy-season humidity is unbearably unpleasant.
After overtime I'm dying for a cold beer.

connection: V-て / adj-くて + しかたがない
nuance: A feeling or sensation is overwhelming and there's nothing to be done about it — everyday register, like てたまらない.

examples.en: Before my first interview I couldn't help feeling extremely nervous.
Everything about the new neighborhood is so novel I can't get enough of it.

nuance: Direct advice or a strong recommendation — the best thing to do is X. Speaker telling listener what's wise.

examples.en: If you want to get good at Japanese, you should speak it every day.
On typhoon days, you shouldn't go out unnecessarily.

connection: question word + plain form + ことか
nuance: An emotional exclamation of degree — how much, how many times, how long. Paired with どんなに / 何度 / どれほど.

examples.en: How much I've grown over this one year!
How many times I got lost on this hilly road!

nuance: Front-loads the speaker's emotional reaction before the fact — to my surprise, to my delight, sadly. Fixed emotion words.

examples.en: To my surprise, my neighbor was also from Hokkaido.
To my regret, the fireworks festival was cancelled due to rain.

nuance: A hedged, reluctant yes via a double negative — it's not that I can't, i.e. I could, with reservations.

examples.en: It's not that I can't eat natto, but every morning would be too much.
It's not that I couldn't take the overtime, but I have plans tonight.

nuance: Literary negative — a firm resolve not to do (I'll never) or a negative guess (surely not). Stiff, written register.

examples.en: I resolve never to make the same mistake again.
With weather like this, the train probably won't be delayed.

connection: V-dict / N-の + 恐れがある
nuance: A formal warning of an undesirable possibility — used in news and official notices for hazards and bad outcomes.

examples.en: There is a risk the trains will stop due to the typhoon.
There is a risk the renewal will be delayed due to a flaw in the documents.
